Title: Customer uploads ID
User Story: As a customer, I want to upload two pieces of ID through the portal so that I can verify my identity and access my account without calling an agent.
Business Value: Removes manual ID collection; reduces agent handling time.
Acceptance Criteria: Customer is shown accepted file formats (PDF, JPG, PNG) and size limit before uploading | Upload confirmation is displayed immediately on screen | Uploaded documents are linked to the customer record securely | Agent is notified of the submission automatically
Dependencies: Data migration (customer record must exist in DB)
Notes or Assumptions: Confirm accepted formats and max file size with Priya. Acceptance criteria are observable by a tester without knowledge of the underlying system.
Priority Rationale: First customer-facing action in the To-Be workflow. Without ID upload, no customer can enter the verified journey. Directly enables Stories 2–4 and unblocks the entire Access & ID board.

Title: Agent reviews and confirms or rejects ID
User Story: As an agent, I want to review a customer's uploaded ID documents alongside their account data and confirm or reject their identity in one screen so that verification is accurate, fast, and fully recorded.
Business Value: Replaces off-system checking; creates compliance audit trail.
Acceptance Criteria: Agent can see uploaded documents and account data on the same screen without switching views | Agent can select confirm or reject; rejection requires a reason selected from a predefined list | A confirmation or rejection is reflected in the customer's account status immediately | Customer receives an automated notification of the outcome | Every action is recorded with the agent's ID and a timestamp
Notes or Assumptions: Without this story no customer can progress past verification. Predefined rejection reasons to be agreed with Gareth and Daniel.
Priority Rationale: Completes the verification loop opened by Story 1. No customer can access self-service without agent confirmation. TCF requires a human decision point here — cannot be automated away.

Title: Customer retries after rejection
User Story: As a customer, I want to resubmit my ID after a rejection with the rejection reason clearly displayed so that I can correct my mistake and complete verification without contacting an agent.
Business Value: Reduces drop-off and agent contact at the verification stage.
Acceptance Criteria: Rejection reason is visible to the customer at the point of resubmission | Customer's personal details are pre-populated; only document upload is repeated | Each retry is recorded as a distinct submission event | Customer is informed of the retry limit before they reach it | When the retry limit is reached the customer is shown a clear next step
Notes or Assumptions: Retry limit to be confirmed with Daniel (compliance). Acceptance criteria are user-observable — testers should not need system access to verify them.
Priority Rationale: Retry failure is the primary drop-off risk at the verification stage. Without this, rejected customers have no self-service route and will call agents — directly undermining the volume reduction case.

Title: Verification audit log
User Story: As a compliance officer, I want every identity verification action to be automatically recorded with a timestamp and the ID of the person who took the action so that the bank can produce a complete audit trail on request.
Business Value: Directly satisfies TCF compliance requirement.
Acceptance Criteria: All submission, review, confirm, reject, and retry events are logged without manual input | Each log entry contains: action type, actor ID, timestamp, and customer reference | Logs cannot be edited or deleted after creation | Logs can be exported as a CSV file
Notes or Assumptions: Immutability requirement to be confirmed with Daniel. This story has no UI — it is a background data requirement and should be verified by a tester inspecting the log output directly.
Priority Rationale: TCF and FCA audit trail requirement. Non-negotiable for Phase 1 regardless of workflow position. Low build complexity relative to compliance risk of omitting it.

Title: Agent receives full context on handoff
User Story: As an agent, I want to see a customer's full account history, verification status, and interaction log the moment I receive a handoff so that I have everything I need to help them without asking them to repeat information.
Business Value: Directly resolves the repeat-information pain point evidenced in the As-Is map.
Acceptance Criteria: On receiving a handoff the agent sees: verification status, full contact log, previous agent notes, P2P status, and follow-up timestamps | All data is drawn from the hybrid DB and reflects the current state of the account | The handoff panel is fully loaded within 3 seconds of the handoff being triggered | If any data fails to load the agent sees a clear error state rather than a blank or partial panel
Notes or Assumptions: Consider splitting into two stories: (1) handoff panel displays core account status; (2) handoff panel displays full interaction history. Central to Gareth's handoff quality concern.
Priority Rationale: The handoff context panel is the single most evidenced pain point in the As-Is map. Resolving repeat-information requests is central to both agent satisfaction and customer experience. Enables Stories 6 and 7.

Title: Agent logs interaction notes
User Story: As an agent, I want to log a structured note after every customer interaction so that the next agent or manager who picks up the case has accurate context without relying on memory or external spreadsheets.
Business Value: Eliminates the manual notes problem flagged throughout the As-Is map.
Acceptance Criteria: Agent can record interaction type, outcome, and next action in a structured form | Note is saved against the customer record with the agent's ID and a timestamp | Saved notes are visible to other agents and managers in the account history view | Notes cannot be deleted once saved
Notes or Assumptions: Directly replaces the manual notes and spreadsheet reconciliation behaviour evidenced in the As-Is map.
Priority Rationale: Replaces the manual notes and spreadsheet reconciliation behaviour that currently causes data loss between agents. Must be in place before Story 7 (manager visibility) is meaningful.

Title: Manager searches and reviews account history
User Story: As a manager, I want to search for any customer account and view its full interaction history so that I can audit cases and give informed support to agents handling complex accounts.
Business Value: Addresses Gareth's visibility concern; enables quality assurance.
Acceptance Criteria: Manager can search by customer name, account reference, or case status | Search returns a full chronological history including notes, verification events, and payment activity | Manager access is logged with a timestamp | Search results load within 5 seconds
Notes or Assumptions: Dependent on Stories 5 and 6 being complete and populated with real data before this story is meaningful in testing.
Priority Rationale: Dependent on Stories 5–6 being complete and populated with real data. Valuable for Gareth's visibility concern but not a customer-facing launch requirement. Medium priority reflects sequencing, not importance.

Title: Customer views account summary dashboard
User Story: As a customer, I want to see my current balance, account status, and available self-service options when I log in so that I can understand my position and take action without speaking to an agent.
Business Value: Reduces inbound contact for basic balance queries; sets context for all self-service actions.
Acceptance Criteria: Dashboard displays: outstanding balance, account status, last payment date, and available self-service options | All figures reflect the current state of the account | Dashboard loads within 3 seconds of login | If data cannot be retrieved the customer sees a clear error message rather than missing or stale figures
Dependencies: Data migration | Access & ID (customer must be verified)
Notes or Assumptions: Gateway to all self-service functionality. Data must be served from the hybrid DB — no legacy spreadsheet reads.
Priority Rationale: Gateway to all self-service functionality. The customer-facing dashboard is the first thing a verified customer sees — every self-service story (9–11) depends on it. High value, moderate build scope.

Title: Customer makes a promise-to-pay (P2P)
User Story: As a customer, I want to commit to a future payment date through the portal so that my repayment intention is recorded without me needing to speak to an agent.
Business Value: Automates high-volume P2P recording; eliminates manual plan note logging from As-Is map.
Acceptance Criteria: Customer can select a payment date within a defined allowable window | The P2P commitment is recorded against the account immediately with a timestamp | Customer receives a confirmation notification after committing | The P2P status and date are visible to agents and managers in the account history view
Notes or Assumptions: One of the highest-volume self-service actions in the To-Be map. Allowable date window to be confirmed with Gareth and Daniel.
Priority Rationale: Highest-volume self-service action in the To-Be map. P2P automation directly reduces agent time spent logging manual plan notes — one of the clearest qualitative efficiency gains in Phase 1.

Title: Customer views and selects a payment plan
User Story: As a customer, I want to view the payment plan options I am eligible for and select one so that I can begin structured repayment without negotiating with an agent.
Business Value: Reduces agent time on straightforward plan setup; accelerates recovery.
Acceptance Criteria: Portal displays only plans the customer is eligible for based on their account data | Customer can select and confirm a plan in a single flow | The selected plan is recorded against the account and visible to agents | If no eligible plans exist the customer is offered the option to speak to an agent
Notes or Assumptions: Eligibility criteria must be confirmed with Daniel before build. Directly supports revenue recovery objective.
Priority Rationale: Directly supports recovery by enabling customers to self-select repayment plans without agent negotiation. Eligibility logic is a dependency risk — confirm criteria with Daniel before build.

Title: Customer requests to speak to an agent (S2A)
User Story: As a customer, I want to request to speak to an agent from within the portal at any point in my journey so that I can get help without leaving the portal and having to repeat my account details.
Business Value: Keeps customers in the portal ecosystem; ensures agent receives full context on connection.
Acceptance Criteria: S2A option is available at every key decision point in the self-service flow | Selecting S2A packages the customer's current context and account data for the receiving agent | Customer is shown an expected wait time or offered a callback option | The S2A request is logged with a timestamp against the customer record
Notes or Assumptions: TCF safeguard — this option must never be hidden or difficult to reach. Named path on the To-Be map.
Priority Rationale: TCF safeguard and named path on the To-Be map. Must be available at every self-service decision point. Without it, customers with complex needs have no route to help — compliance and reputational risk.

Title: Portal routes S2A request to available agent
User Story: As an agent, I want to receive an S2A request with the customer's full context already loaded so that I can begin helping them immediately without asking them to repeat information.
Business Value: Resolves the messy handoff problem central to Gareth's concern.
Acceptance Criteria: Agent receives a notification within 2 minutes of the customer submitting an S2A request | Notification includes the customer's name, account reference, and a summary of their portal journey so far | If no agent is available the customer is offered a callback with an estimated time | Every routing decision is logged with a timestamp
Notes or Assumptions: Reframed from system actor to agent perspective. Handoff quality is the most evidenced operational risk in the project.
Priority Rationale: The technical mechanism behind Story 11. Handoff quality is the most evidenced operational risk in the project — Gareth's core concern. Context packaging at routing point is what prevents repeat-information requests.

Title: System flags high-risk accounts for escalation
User Story: As an agent, I want to be automatically assigned accounts that have been flagged as high-risk so that they are never left in the standard self-service queue without specialist attention.
Business Value: Directly maps to the marked as high risk path on As-Is process map; protects revenue and compliance.
Acceptance Criteria: Accounts meeting the high-risk criteria are routed to the specialist queue without manual intervention | The criteria that triggered the flag are visible to the receiving agent | The customer is notified that their case is being handled by a specialist without being shown the risk classification | Flag thresholds are configurable by a manager without developer involvement | Every routing decision is logged
Notes or Assumptions: Consider splitting into: (1) system applies high-risk flag based on configurable criteria; (2) flagged accounts are routed to specialist queue. Flagging criteria to be agreed with Daniel, Gareth, and Amina before build. Evidenced in As-Is map only — not explicitly in To-Be.
Priority Rationale: Compliance and revenue risk if high-risk accounts enter standard self-service flow. Added to scope post-review. Flagging criteria must be agreed with Daniel, Gareth, and Amina before build — do not assume defaults.

Title: Agent logs customer abandonment
User Story: As an agent, I want to record when a customer abandons an interaction so that the account is flagged for follow-up and the event is not lost.
Business Value: Closes the gap in the As-Is map where abandonment was invisible to managers.
Acceptance Criteria: Agent can mark an interaction as abandoned and select a reason from a predefined list | Abandonment is logged against the customer record with the agent's ID and a timestamp | The account is automatically added to the follow-up schedule after an abandonment is logged | Abandoned cases are visible to managers in the account history view
Notes or Assumptions: Important for reporting accuracy and follow-up rate metrics. Not a customer-facing launch blocker.
Priority Rationale: Important for follow-up accuracy and reporting completeness, but does not block any customer-facing journey. Scheduled for post-launch or late Phase 1 sprint. Feeds the missed follow-up metric directly.

Title: Manager views real-time verification queue
User Story: As a manager, I want to see a live count of ID verifications by status so that I can spot agent workload imbalances and act before a backlog develops.
Business Value: Addresses Gareth's visibility concern with direct evidence from the As-Is map.
Acceptance Criteria: Dashboard displays counts for: pending, in review, confirmed, and rejected verifications | Data refreshes at least every 5 minutes | Manager can click through from a status count to the individual cases behind it | Dashboard is accessible without operational system permissions
Notes or Assumptions: Dependent on Stories 1–3 generating real data. Not required at customer-facing launch but should follow shortly after.
Priority Rationale: Supports Gareth's visibility need and enables proactive workload management. Not required at customer-facing launch but should follow closely. Dependent on Stories 1–3 generating real data.

Title: Executive views recovery and portal adoption metrics
User Story: As an executive, I want a high-level view of portal adoption and recovery performance so that I can assess whether Phase 1 is delivering its intended outcomes without needing access to operational systems.
Business Value: Satisfies Daniel's 12-month ROI requirement; supports executive reinforcement.
Acceptance Criteria: Dashboard displays: total accounts managed via portal, recovery conversion rate, average handling time, and missed follow-up rate | All figures are exportable as CSV | Dashboard is accessible with executive-level permissions only | Data reflects activity within a selectable date range
Notes or Assumptions: Potentially out of Phase 1 scope. Recommend a lightweight version at the 30-day post-launch review. Without this, executive disengagement post-launch is a confirmed change risk.
Priority Rationale: Potentially out of Phase 1 scope per updated notes. Qualitatively, executive visibility of adoption metrics is the primary reinforcement mechanism for sustained portal use — without it, disengagement risk is high. Recommend a lightweight version at 30-day post-launch review.

Title: Customer notified to visit portal
User Story: As a customer, I want to receive a notification when action is needed on my account so that I know to log into the portal without waiting for an agent to contact me.
Business Value: Drives portal adoption; reduces agent outbound contact volume.
Acceptance Criteria: Notification is sent via the customer's preferred channel (email or SMS) | Notification includes a clear description of the required action and a direct link to the portal | Delivery and open events are recorded against the customer record | Notifications are not sent outside of agreed contact hours
Dependencies: Data migration (customer contact preferences stored in DB)
Notes or Assumptions: Portal visits in the To-Be map are frequently notification-triggered. Customer contact preferences must be stored in the hybrid DB at migration.
Priority Rationale: Portal visits in the To-Be map are frequently notification-triggered. Without outbound prompts, customer adoption depends entirely on self-initiated logins — significantly weakening the volume reduction case. High priority despite being a Notifications story.

Title: Agent receives automated follow-up reminder
User Story: As an agent, I want to receive an automated reminder when a follow-up is due on an account so that I never miss a scheduled contact.
Business Value: Directly targets the 14% missed follow-up rate evidenced by Finance Assumption 06.
Acceptance Criteria: Reminder is triggered automatically on the scheduled follow-up date | Reminder displays: customer name, account reference, and a summary of the last interaction | Agent can mark the reminder as actioned, snooze it, or escalate the case | Reminders that are not actioned within a configurable window are escalated to the agent's manager
Dependencies: Account history | Self-service (P2P dates feed follow-up schedule)
Notes or Assumptions: Directly targets the 14% missed follow-up rate from Finance Assumption 06. Follow-up dates are fed by P2P commitments (Story 9) — dependency must be in place before this story is testable end-to-end.
Priority Rationale: The 14% missed follow-up rate is the most concrete evidence of operational waste in the dossier. Automating reminders is a direct and qualitatively strong efficiency gain. Depends on P2P dates (Story 9) being written to the DB.

Title: Legacy customer records migrated to hybrid DB
User Story: As a system administrator, I want all existing customer account records migrated to the hybrid database before launch so that every other part of the portal has a complete and accurate data foundation to work from.
Business Value: Phase 1 gate condition per the dossier — nothing else works without this.
Acceptance Criteria: A minimum of 80% of records are migrated without errors | Duplicate records are identified and resolved before migration completes | A migration audit log is produced showing: total records, error count, error types, and resolution actions | No portal story is tested in integration until migration is confirmed complete
Dependencies: None — foundational dependency for all other areas
Notes or Assumptions: Phase 1 gate condition. Blocks every other story. Must begin immediately and run in parallel with all other build activity.
Priority Rationale: Absolute Phase 1 gate condition. No other story can function without clean, migrated customer records in the hybrid DB. Must begin immediately and run in parallel with all other build activity. 80% cleanliness threshold is non-negotiable per dossier.

Title: Data validation checks run pre-migration
User Story: As a system administrator, I want automated validation checks run against legacy data before migration begins so that dirty or incomplete records are identified and cleaned before they enter the hybrid database.
Business Value: Protects data integrity; reduces post-migration errors across all boards.
Acceptance Criteria: Validation checks identify: duplicate records, missing mandatory fields, and formatting errors | A validation report is produced showing error count by category | Migration cannot proceed until the validation pass rate meets the 80% threshold | Validation can be re-run after cleaning without restarting the full process
Notes or Assumptions: Precondition for Story 19. Significantly cheaper to fix data quality issues here than post-migration.
Priority Rationale: Precondition for Story 19. Validation before migration prevents dirty data propagating into the new system — far cheaper to catch errors here than post-migration. Should be the first technical task initiated.

Title: Portal reads live data from hybrid DB
User Story: As a user (agent/customer), I want the data I see in the portal to always reflect the current state of an account so that I can make decisions based on accurate information.
Business Value: Eliminates the unreconciled data problem causing repeat customer questions and missed follow-ups.
Acceptance Criteria: All data displayed in the portal is served from the hybrid DB with no reads from legacy spreadsheets | Data displayed is no more than 10 seconds old | If a data fetch fails the user sees a clear error message rather than stale or missing data | No cached or default values are shown in place of real account data
Notes or Assumptions: Reframed from system actor to agent/customer perspective. Without this story functioning correctly no other portal story can be tested accurately in integration.
Priority Rationale: Without live DB reads, the portal serves stale or missing data — every customer-facing and agent-facing story breaks. Technically foundational; must be proven before any frontend stories are tested in integration.

Title: Interaction data written back to hybrid DB in real time
User Story: As a user (agent/customer), I want every action I take in the portal to be saved immediately so that nothing is lost and anyone picking up the case afterwards has a complete and current picture.
Business Value: Creates the single source of truth that resolves the spreadsheet reconciliation problem at the core of the As-Is map.
Acceptance Criteria: All interaction types — payments, P2Ps, notes, verifications, S2A requests — are written to the hybrid DB within 5 seconds of completion | A write failure triggers an alert visible to a system administrator | No interaction is silently discarded; failed writes are queued and retried | The saved record is immediately visible to other users with access to that account
Notes or Assumptions: Reframed from system actor to agent/customer perspective. Underpins data integrity across the entire system — this is the mechanism that creates the single source of truth.
Priority Rationale: Closes the write-back loop that creates the single source of truth. Without this, the portal reads correctly but interactions are not persisted — recreating the unreconciled data problem the system is designed to solve.
